excerpt: [Laughter] welcome to the ascetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're talking about the important topic of transgender clients and what you need to understand about sexual dimorphism to give them the best possible care okay so this topic i mean w…

segments.text: [Laughter] welcome to the ascetics mastery show i'm dr tim pearce hi i'm miranda pierce and today we're talking about the important topic of transgender clients and what you need to understand about sexual dimorphism to give them the best possible care okay so this topic i mean we treat a fair number of trans clients and it's something that i think is maybe not understood very well by a lot of people but when peop but i think clinicians who i speak to are very keen to get into this because they know that they could really help so has that been your experience that it has been something that's been quite transformative for them yes it can be in immensely rewarding but it also has complexities because it's a difficult situation and it depends on the individual that you're faced with so

this is the key for me which is there is no one category called transgender clients they are all individuals with very unique and different stories which are really worth listening to and so that you design a treatment that fits that individual which is no different to what we say for all our patients really in fact you can learn an enormous amount about how to do good aesthetic treatments from doing a really good job with transgender people because the same principles apply to everyone understand their story understand their perceptions design the treatment exactly around the words that they use the things that they say that matter to them and um be careful you know i mean careful with their psychology we should be doing this with all our patients but it's very easy not to and people who are superficially more simple so um the time that you tend to that you spend understanding an individual in the case of a transgender client most clinicians are automatically a bit more aware that they need to talk and listen and understand before they plow in with an injection plan and that's actually what we should do with all our patients in many ways let's start with sexual dimorphism

which is help me out sexual dimorphism thank you which is basically the difference between what um wherever someone's on their journey the difference between a man and a woman physically yeah so we are we're all finely tuned to recognize masculine and feminine features i mean it you can see someone some sometimes actually i'm amazed you can tell from a speck in the distance if it's a male or a female gate like we all understand that there's a difference in the geometry of how your bones move and similar with faces if you imagine a quick sketch of a face you could probably create a masculine flavored sketch with two lines and our brains would go yeah that's a man and that's because we're finally really finely tuned to detecting that is really important for our survival that we can tell who are male and who are female and that's woven into our dna and it's part of how we survive that we recognize and place people quickly obviously there are issues with it and it gets complex and doesn't fit everyone but that's essentially how our brains but that's essentially how our brains work okay so we need to understand and make conscious this is what aesthetics

is all about we have to be the experts on the aesthetic and how it fits with how people look and feel so you're trying to make conscious in our minds what uh what masculine is versus what feminine is because when it's conscious you can control it for most of us it's actually unconscious most people you don't have to think about whether it's a male or female it's not an intellectual thing you know instinctually but we as clinicians who are altering these variables need to be sure exactly what we're altering and why so let's make it conscious tell me about the difference between men and women in the different parts of the faith yeah so let's start as we do in a good assessment or a good treatment you normally start from the top and work your way down um lateral to medial the first thing that's different for male and female face is probably just a hairline so mostly you'll see men have a squarer hairline so their hair grows back and in fact we probably fit this quite well ourselves so your hair goes up a little bit higher at all ages two degree obviously it gets more so um and uh and then also you have a

broader forehead as well so the actual bones are bigger so you're actually projected out more laterally low down the ten the frontal eminence in females is actually more forward so you you'll look at the middle of a female forehead and that central projection is slightly more anterior than it is in a male we are slightly more slanted back and that's probably partly because our periodical ridge is more projected so we have these bones here that are uh if you picture neanderthal it's a very masculine image like it when although there were neanderthal females we all picture the man and it's this really powerful ridge why did you guys not evolve not carry on no it's not that we're neanderthals it's just that neanderthals were masculine in fact most people have somebody i think i do have point one percent neanderthal i did test it but you probably do as well but the point is not so much about the dna it's just the shape we all understand that image of the caveman and it's that's the most masculine archetype almost and that's what you can see in a very masculine phase is a strong

period or a strong brow ridge okay um so you it's also all the bones are bigger essentially that's kind of what you're sensing in a masculine phase is there's this hypertrophy of all the bony elements so noses are bigger brow ridges bigger foreheads are bigger uh wider so after the brow ridge and the next thing to think about in male and female faces is the shape of the eyebrow so in most ethnicities the arch of an eyebrow is a feminine signal now you do get men with an arch but it tends to go more with female faces and it's there's an angle kind of 11 to 14 degrees where the the hairline goes up and then reaches the peak now in men that tends to be flatter and then the eyebrows drop off towards the edge um and that's um that's probably one of the things we do most often with toxin is try and elevate the arch and if you do own a man and this has happened to various celebrities i think um you i saw shane warne it's always the sportsman that we noticed so shane warne um who's the other one really wayne rooney had a treatment and you could see

this new arch that develops and it and it's it can either look feminine or it can look slightly sinister unfortunately joseph fritzel awful uh man and gary litter as well it often has this sinister element which is why men most men don't actually like that and uh and well they will tend to ask for you to flatten their brow ridge if it accidentally lifts and unfortunately we all do this often when you first start in your career and you've been taught how to do female faces and you apply the same logic to a male face you end up making this mistake and then the poor guy comes back and says please can you soften my eyebrows so um but that's exactly why because the sexual dimorphism it confuses us it doesn't look right we can all tell there's something slightly wrong about this situation um so yeah that's the top half the you've then got the midface so midface is all about the cheeks being relatively smaller compared to the rest of the face in a male face and a in a female face they're they are dominant they're the most wide part of the face and on either side in a female face you're kind of talking

10 to 12 degrees for to line in with the temples um and it's probably about 10 degrees lining in with the gonial angle so there's that v-shape in females now in male faces if you have a look at a picture of brad pitt for example his cheek is completely flush with his jawline and in really masculine faces they can actually be the other way around so you have this projection of the jaw actually wider than the cheeks and we all sense that immediately as in fact i think it's perceived as aggression it actually looks slightly aggressive and we all feel that you actually feel differently when you meet someone like that they not that they're being aggressive but you're a little bit more cautious um in those situations so this is um that's the relationship between the midface and lower face now obviously men have also have bigger noses and that's partly because they're bigger chests and they need to breathe more so they're built for more physical stuff so that has to be supported through the airway so you get bigger noses and lips tend to be smaller and wider so they tend to be narrower and um

and relatively wide and flat rather than females are a little bit more projected for lips for whatever reason are section secondary sexual characteristics they are and they change color when women are fertile and that's why it tends to be that women have their lips done and now men can have their lips done and it's also part of male beauty but it's a different in a really masculine face they're not a big feature so what you may this is where we'll get on to the topic which is not everyone fits the black and white mold and you do need to have feminine characteristics and male faces and sometimes masculine characteristics and female phases for someone to feel in their essence which is what we're always trying to do so this is where you can start to play with the variables and it does make sense sometimes to feminize a face and some of the men having treatments on their lips will want a hint of femininity but then also it doesn't necessarily mean they want to look like a woman so it's well this is where the conversation needs to be had in a really delicate and careful way so um we always think about the intercanthal distance being the foundation of the golden ratio but it's quite useful in terms of

understanding the male and female characteristics so you should have a slightly wider space in men and just because their heads are bigger but at the same time nose still fits in the intercontinental distance and then it should in a female lines up with your chin so your chin is as wide as your nose now in a male the chin tends to be the same width as the mouth or at least the golden ratio to the intercontinental distance so you have a wider square of chin and you can see that we actually fit that quite well i have a relatively flat chin it's relatively broad and you have a chin that's pointed and fits in with the width of your nose so that's how you can all tell that i'm male and you're female so and this is all stuff that happens obviously in the blink of an eye what are the other things so jawline very jawline i always think of in terms of male beauty that jawline is like cheeks are for women so you do want the jaw to be more dominant in a male face and cheeks to be more dominant in a female face and jawlines are um it's the angle here is sharper so you'll go in your anger angle tends to be more a less

a more acute angle in a male and it's obviously projected outwards more because it needs to match your cheek so it's slightly broader and then some really subtle stuff that goes with masculinity is to do with the outward curve of the jaw so if you're projecting the jawline out when you're injecting you're making someone more masculine which is probably one of the things you see quite sadly happening to a lot of clients who want a more defined jawline is they also end up getting a more masculine jawline so the pictures look amazing from the side because you have that wonderful shadow but as soon as the head looks straight at you see this extra projection because it's very hard to get definition without adding volume and if you add volume to the width of your jaw you look more masculine let me ask you when transgender patients present is there any trend in terms of do you find that there's more um trans people transitioning from male to female or female to male um i think do i i can't actually i think i've probably more i've seen more male to female transitions but

transitions but i've definitely seen both and i've also seen people who are not transitioning at all but they actually had an interesting request to look in between um what's the technical term androgynous androgenous so that was really interesting because there are all these variables and you have to kind of match take a little bit from each part of the face and tweak it just enough so that it's it loses its femininity or increases its masculinity whatever in whichever way that you want to create that sense of uncertainty effectively um so that was really interesting intellectually to try and figure out and talk through that patient and try and give them an idea of what it would look like that matched what they were hoping to feel like so i used photoshop for that quite a lot and that works really well because you get the approval of the concept before you stick a needle in and do you find that with trans people that they are that they come to you at a particular part of their transition um i think i've got i've seen the whole spectrum from i'm used to this

i'm 20 years into it two i've only just told my parents and i'm just looking to get this additional experience so those are the two experience the two extremes from just broaching the topic openly to i'm well i'm completely over this and i'm just powering on through and there's no fear yeah no uncertainty just like i would come and get my lips done i'm just looking to enhance what i already believe i am and you know just want more of me out in the world yeah exactly okay so tell us then with um i'm interested in someone who is you know quite deep into transitioning maybe even completely and so they're just looking to change their look and to help them with that do is there anything that we need to think of in terms of you know let's say someone who is on a particular uh hormonal treatment are there any kind of do you have any contraindications or anything like that anything practical and i think the usual principles

would apply which is you don't want to be doing elective procedures at a point of uncertainty so if you've just started a new medication or just about to have surgery you would suggest leaving it until a point where things are more stable before you start to make unnecessary changes now sometimes it's part of the decision making process like i just want to see if this will make me happy and then i might go and do something else and i mean this is where it gets really complex but that is a possible reason why you might do it in a slightly uncertain time as almost as a diagnostic and a therapeutic tool for some people but all needs to be spoken out at length to make sure that you understand exactly the purpose at that particular time but there are there is nothing black and white contraindication that i'm aware of other than it's more about the psychological contraindications that we need to be careful of okay and when someone is transitioning say or they're on their journey from um formerly you know society

thinking that they're a woman to becoming a man do they need lots of filler because there's obviously jawline can be quite a big treatment yes well who would regardless of whether it's male to female or female to male to completely to do a really good shift often will take more product but you should also be aware and it's also worth saying this in the consultation that we can't with injections control all the variables so they will and most of them most people would know this you might need a surgical component to achieve the result you really want so a good consultation would cover that but they're basically all going to be fairly large volumes because you're trying to make a really obvious difference i mean we accidentally feminize and masculinize people in treatments fairly often like we're talking about with jawlines you can accidentally put a bit too much and it can look slightly masculine and we and people pick it up but then they don't look like the opposite gender they just have a flavor and what we're actually trying to do is if you're

actually trying to fully cross that divide into the looking like the opposite gender it's much higher volumes so all of these treatments are much higher volumes which is also something kind of socially to think about which is that you may want to have a different pricing structure for example because it makes it so hard and they someone in that i think there's quite a lot of evidence that transgender people tend to not everyone and not everyone fits the mold for anything but they tend to be held back financially because they're dealing with other issues and so um something that we've certainly unofficially have done is just have make it easier financially to get the result that they really want having said that though i think it's you already said that people who are transitioning maybe require much more let's say i don't know what the right word is but kind of counseling to make sure this is right for them so you know i'm already imagining you know are we doubling consultation times are we kind of taking that into account

are we thinking more follow-ups you know i don't want to make it all about money but i think that's important to sort of acknowledge that this is not a simple you know bishop brush get your lips done and you're out okay so you're asking um like what's the risk for clinicians if suddenly you have 500 transgender people and they all want longer appointments and paying less at the extreme end um i think it's obviously really unlikely because a small number of people um and if that was to were to happen you should always structure your prices that so that you defend the foundations of your business and le and one of the i mean one of the ways of doing that is you might narrow the number of appointments available like i do you could do it almost something an idea i played with is almost have you know there's one person a month that i do at a as part of um my business as positive branding but also to help a certain cohort of or you know could be someone who's unwell or someone who's transgender it doesn't matter what it was but you could narrow it in terms of the number of appointments that you offer that are

effectively subsidized okay um that's one way to do it but i think that's a good point which is if you know you may love this field of work and find it incredibly interesting and find that you want to do more of it but realize that if you multiplied the appointments out that you would actually be going out of business soon that's no good to anyone so you need to do the maths and find a way to do it in a sustainable way okay and have you experienced with a trans patient who's either you know male to female to male is there one particular treatment that just really helps move someone forward that they kind of think oh thank god you know it's me i'm sitting looking at me i don't i can't think of one specifically i mean there are it's probably easier to masculinize a feminine face than feminize a masculine phase okay um so because it's effectively male faces are bigger so it's the one where you're adding volume so it's a little bit easier to give someone to square off someone's chin or jawline than it is to make someone's cheeks look full and round without them

not fitting in some other way so in that way it's kind of aesthetically easier um obviously the ultimate depends what you like but the thing i love most of all is whoever feels and it doesn't have to be transgender whoever feels amazing in their skin after they leave and you can normally tell it's the mirror moment when someone looks in the mirror and i believe what people are seeing is who they think they should be like it's that moment of yes that's me it's a moment of self recognition and you can normally see that before they say anything you see that moment of self recognition and you know that they're happy that's the most important thing but that can be anyone that doesn't it doesn't have to be a someone who's transgender but if i see that with somebody's transgender then yes you feel like i've had a worthwhile day today yeah that's gorgeous yeah so i hope you've found that interesting i know we talked a little bit about the male and female faces in a previous treatment and the ideal male face which by the way i understand that my videographer was sneaking his own face if you know where that clip is and you see the frame and he does it again
